criteria: Server creates a TCP socket with socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0) and checks the return value for -1
SO_REUSEADDR socket option is set on the server socket before bind() to allow immediate restart without TIME_WAIT delay
Server binds to a configurable port (default 8080) using bind() with htons() applied to the port number in struct sockaddr_in
listen() is called with a backlog of at least 128 to queue incoming connections
Server runs an infinite accept loop; accept() errors log and continue without terminating the server process
Each accepted client_fd is closed with close() after the response is fully sent, with no FD leaks across sequential connections
Request reading uses a recv() loop that accumulates bytes into a fixed 8192-byte buffer and continues until the \r\n\r\n end-of-headers delimiter is found
recv() returning 0 (client orderly shutdown) is handled as a non-crash condition that terminates the current request gracefully
Buffer size limit is enforced: reading stops and the connection is dropped if 8191 bytes are received without finding \r\n\r\n
Server sends a hardcoded HTTP/1.1 200 OK response with correct Content-Type, Content-Length (matching actual body byte count), and Connection: close headers, followed by \r\n\r\n and an HTML body
Response is sent with a send_all() loop to handle partial writes, using MSG_NOSIGNAL or equivalent to suppress SIGPIPE
SIGPIPE is handled at startup via signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN) or MSG_NOSIGNAL on all send() calls so that client disconnection during write does not terminate the server process
Server accepts a port number as an optional command-line argument and falls back to 8080 if none is provided
curl -v http://localhost:PORT/ returns HTTP/1.1 200 OK with the correct headers and HTML body
Server survives rapid sequential connections (1000+) without hitting EMFILE (file descriptor leak test)
Parse the request line extracting method, path, and HTTP version using memchr()-based scanning that is bounds-safe and does not read past buf_len bytes
Reject malformed request lines (missing spaces, empty path, unrecognized version string) with a 400 Bad Request response containing a valid HTML body and correct Content-Length
Enforce a maximum path length of 8192 bytes and return 414 URI Too Long for paths at or exceeding this limit, before any path processing occurs
Store all header names normalized to lowercase at parse time so that lookups via get_header() use plain strcmp() with lowercase keys
Strip optional whitespace (spaces and tabs) from both ends of header values before storing them
Accept both CRLF (\r\n) and bare LF (\n) line terminators and produce identical parse results for both
Detect and unfold obsolete obs-fold header continuations (lines beginning with space or tab) by appending the continuation to the previous header value with a single space separator
Return a pointer to the request body (pointing into the raw read buffer) and correctly compute body_len as the number of bytes between the end of the empty header line and the end of the buffer
Read remaining body bytes from the socket using a recv() loop when Content-Length exceeds bytes already captured in the header buffer
Extract the Host header value and log it; handle its absence with at minimum a logged warning
Respond with 501 Not Implemented for any method other than GET and HEAD, with a valid HTML error body
HEAD responses return the identical set of response headers as GET but with a zero-byte body — the same code path with a send_body flag controls this distinction
The http_request_t struct is fully initialized (memset to zero) before parsing begins, preventing use of uninitialized fields
The parse_headers function correctly handles MAX_HEADERS limit by stopping silently when the array is full rather than overflowing
Header names longer than 127 bytes are rejected or skipped rather than causing a buffer overflow
url_decode() is called on req->path before concatenating with the document root; percent-encoded sequences like %2e%2e%2f are decoded to ../  prior to any path operations
Decoded path is validated to contain no null bytes (\u0000); a path containing %00 returns 400 Bad Request
realpath() is called on the concatenated path before the prefix containment check; symlinks inside the document root that point outside it are caught and return 403 Forbidden
The prefix containment check verifies that canonical[strlen(doc_root)] is either '/' or '\0', preventing /var/www/html2 from matching /var/www/html
Directory paths trigger an index.html lookup (appending /index.html to the canonical path); if index.html does not exist, the server returns 403 Forbidden, not 404
realpath() is re-called after appending /index.html to the directory canonical path, and the containment check is re-run on the result

403 Forbidden is returned for any path whose canonical form does not begin with the document root, regardless of whether the target file exists — no information about the external filesystem is leaked
Content-Type is detected from the file extension using a case-insensitive lookup table covering at minimum: .html, .htm, .css, .js, .json, .txt, .xml, .svg, .png, .jpg, .jpeg, .gif, .ico, .pdf
text/* MIME types include '; charset=utf-8' in the Content-Type header value
Unknown or missing file extensions produce Content-Type: application/octet-stream
Content-Length header value exactly equals st.st_size as returned by stat(), cast to long long and formatted with %lld
Last-Modified header is formatted in RFC 7231 HTTP-date format (e.g., 'Mon, 01 Jan 2024 12:00:00 GMT') using gmtime() for UTC conversion
Files are read and sent using POSIX open(O_RDONLY) and read() in a loop with a read buffer of at least 4096 bytes; the file descriptor is closed on all exit paths including error paths
404 Not Found with an HTML error body is returned when realpath() fails with ENOENT or when stat() fails after canonicalization
403 Forbidden with an HTML error body is returned when realpath() fails with EACCES or ELOOP, or when open() fails with EACCES
If-Modified-Since header is parsed using strptime() with the RFC 7231 date format and converted to time_t using timegm() (not mktime())
304 Not Modified is returned when a valid If-Modified-Since timestamp is present and st.st_mtime <= the parsed timestamp (less-than-or-equal, one-second granularity)
304 Not Modified response includes Content-Type, Content-Length, and Last-Modified headers but no response body
HEAD requests receive the same headers as GET (including Content-Type, Content-Length, Last-Modified) but no response body
The server does not crash or leak file descriptors when serving 1000 sequential file requests; open FD count returns to baseline after serving

Thread-per-connection model: pthread_create() spawns a detached handler thread for each accepted connection; connection_t is heap-allocated and freed by the thread; pthread_create() errors close the FD without crashing
Thread pool initialization: thread_pool_init() creates exactly N pthreads (default 16, configurable via command-line argument) that block on pthread_cond_wait() until work arrives

Work queue: circular buffer of capacity >= 1024 work_item_t entries protected by a single pthread_mutex_t; head/tail indices wrap using modulo arithmetic; count field accurately reflects items in queue

503 on queue full: when thread_pool_enqueue() finds count == WORK_QUEUE_CAPACITY, it returns -1; caller sends 'HTTP/1.1 503 Service Unavailable' with Retry-After: 1 header and closes the FD without enqueueing
Worker thread correctness: worker loop uses WHILE (not if) around pthread_cond_wait(); checks shutdown flag after waking; dequeues item and releases mutex BEFORE calling handle_client_keep_alive()

HTTP/1.1 keep-alive: handle_client_keep_alive() loops on read_request_with_timeout() + serve; loop continues when should_keep_alive() returns 1 (HTTP/1.1 default or explicit Connection: keep-alive); loop exits when Connection: close received, client closes, or timeout fires

should_keep_alive() logic: returns 0 for HTTP/1.0 without explicit keep-alive header; returns 1 for HTTP/1.1 without explicit close header; explicit Connection header overrides version default for both protocols
Response Connection header: every response includes Connection: keep-alive or Connection: close matching the actual post-response socket behavior
MAX_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION cap: keep-alive loop exits after serving a configurable maximum (>= 100) requests on a single connection

Per-connection idle timeout: read_request_with_timeout() calls select() with tv_sec = 30 (configurable) before each recv(); returns -1 when select() returns 0 (timeout); worker closes connection and becomes available for new work

atomic_int shutdown flag: server_shutdown declared as atomic_int; signal handler sets it with atomic_store(); accept loop checks it with atomic_load(); flag change is visible across threads without data race
Signal handling: SIGTERM and SIGINT both trigger graceful shutdown; SIGPIPE is ignored process-wide; EINTR from accept() causes continue not break

Graceful shutdown sequence: accept loop exits when server_shutdown is set; close(server_fd) called before thread_pool_shutdown(); thread_pool_shutdown() sets pool->shutdown=1, calls pthread_cond_broadcast(), then pthread_join() for every worker thread; process exits only after all joins complete
In-flight request completion: a download in progress at SIGTERM time completes fully (no truncated response) before the process exits
Shared stats mutex: active_connections and total_connections counters are incremented/decremented under pthread_mutex_lock(); no plain unsynchronized int++ on shared counters
Access log thread safety: access_log_write() acquires a mutex, calls fprintf() + fflush(), then releases; concurrent log writes from multiple threads produce complete non-interleaved lines
FD leak test: after 10,000 sequential single-request connections (ab -n 10000 -c 1), /proc/PID/fd count returns to within 5 FDs of baseline
EMFILE recovery: accept() returning EMFILE or ENFILE triggers usleep(100000) and continue, not exit or break
pthread_create failure handling: if pthread_create() fails (returns non-zero), client_fd is closed and conn is freed before continuing the accept loop
Compilation: server compiles with gcc -Wall -Wextra -pthread with zero warnings; links with -lpthread
Server binds to a configurable port (default 8080) and listens for incoming TCP connections
SO_REUSEADDR is set before bind() so the server can restart within 60 seconds without EADDRINUSE
htons() is used for the port number in addr.sin_port — server binds to the correct port
Accept loop handles sequential client connections one at a time without exiting on accept() errors
accept() returning EINTR uses continue, not break — signal does not kill the accept loop
accept() returning EMFILE or ENFILE logs and sleeps briefly before retrying — recoverable FD exhaustion handled
read_request() loops on recv() until \r\n\r\n is found — partial reads handled correctly at any recv() boundary
recv() returning 0 (client EOF before headers complete) causes read_request() to return -1 — no null-dereference
recv() buffer limit (REQUEST_BUF_SIZE - 1) enforced — unbounded reads impossible, null terminator always fits
buf[total] = '\0' written after every recv() call before strstr() — no undefined behavior in delimiter search
Hardcoded HTTP/1.1 200 OK response sent with Content-Type, Content-Length, Connection: close headers
Content-Length header value (27) matches the body byte count exactly — clients do not hang or truncate
send_all() loops until all bytes are sent — partial writes handled correctly
MSG_NOSIGNAL passed to every send() call inside send_all() — SIGPIPE suppressed per call
signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN) called at startup before any socket operations — process-wide defense in depth
close(client_fd) called after handle_client() returns on every path including error paths — no FD leaks
handle_client() never calls close(client_fd) itself — caller (main) owns the close
FD count returns to baseline after 10,000 sequential connections — zero FD leaks verified
Server survives 1,000 mid-response client disconnections without crashing — SIGPIPE fully handled
accept() + recv() + send() + close() round-trip completes in under 1ms on loopback
Parse request line extracting method (GET, HEAD, POST), path, and HTTP version (HTTP/1.1); reject malformed request lines with 400 Bad Request
Parse headers into key-value pairs, handling optional whitespace around header values and case-insensitive header names per RFC 7230
Handle both CRLF (\r\n) and bare LF (\n) line endings for robustness
Extract and log the Host header value (required in HTTP/1.1)
Read request body based on Content-Length header when present (for future POST support)
Respond with 501 Not Implemented for methods other than GET and HEAD
HEAD requests return the same headers as GET but with an empty body
Respond with 414 URI Too Long for request paths exceeding 8KB
Server maps URL path to filesystem path within a configured document root directory
Canonicalize the resolved path using realpath() and verify it is still within the document root; reject with 403 Forbidden if path escapes root
url_decode() applied before path concatenation; percent-encoded traversal sequences such as %2e%2e%2f are decoded then rejected by realpath()+containment check
Null bytes decoded from %00 in the URL path are rejected with 400 Bad Request before reaching the filesystem
Detect and set Content-Type header based on file extension using a MIME type map supporting at least .html .css .js .json .png .jpg .gif .svg .txt .pdf
Send file contents in response body with correct Content-Length header matching st.st_size exactly
Return 404 Not Found for files that do not exist, with a simple HTML error page
Return 403 Forbidden for paths that resolve outside the document root; 403 is returned even when the escaped target path does not exist to prevent information leakage
Support If-Modified-Since header: compare against file modification time using timegm() for UTC correctness and return 304 Not Modified with no body when file is unchanged
Set Last-Modified header on all 200 file responses using the file modification timestamp formatted as RFC 7231 HTTP-date in UTC
Serve index.html automatically when path maps to a directory if index.html exists; otherwise return 403
realpath() is re-run on the index.html path and containment is re-checked to prevent index.html symlinks from escaping the document root
HEAD requests return the same headers as GET including Content-Type Content-Length and Last-Modified but with an empty body
304 response includes Content-Type Content-Length and Last-Modified headers but no body
file_fd opened by serve_file() is closed on every exit path including all error paths
Binary files are read and sent using POSIX open()+read() with O_RDONLY ensuring no newline translation corrupts binary content
Unknown file extensions map to application/octet-stream as the default MIME type
No heap allocation is performed per request; all buffers are stack-allocated
Server binds to a configurable port (default 8080) and accepts concurrent client connections via a bounded thread pool with configurable size (default 16 threads)

Thread pool implements a circular work queue of 1024 slots with pthread_mutex_t protection and two pthread_cond_t variables (not_empty, not_full); worker threads use while loops around pthread_cond_wait to handle spurious wakeups
When the work queue is full, the accept loop sends HTTP/1.1 503 Service Unavailable with Connection: close and Retry-After: 1 headers, closes client_fd immediately, and continues accepting
HTTP/1.1 keep-alive loop handles multiple sequential requests per connection without closing; Connection: close header or HTTP/1.0 default causes server to close after the response
Per-connection idle timeout of 30 seconds (configurable) implemented via select() with timeval before each recv() call; EINTR retries select() with fresh timeval and checks shutdown flag

Graceful shutdown on SIGTERM/SIGINT: atomic_int flag set by async-signal-safe signal handler; accept loop exits on flag; thread_pool_shutdown() broadcasts to all workers and pthread_join drains all in-flight requests before process exits
All in-flight requests complete before server exits on graceful shutdown; binary file downloads are not truncated
Shared connection counter (server_stats_t) protected by its own pthread_mutex_t; access log (access_log_t) protected by a separate pthread_mutex_t with fflush() inside the lock on each write
Zero data races confirmed by ThreadSanitizer (-fsanitize=thread) under concurrent load of 50 clients
File descriptor count returns to baseline after 10,000 sequential connections; no FD leaks on any code path including error paths and timeout paths
pthread_create() failure closes client_fd and logs error without crashing the accept loop; accept() EINTR continues the loop; EMFILE/ENFILE causes 100ms sleep and continue
MAX_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION (100) hard cap prevents a single connection from monopolizing a worker thread indefinitely
